title: Is This What They Call “Protection for Women”?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Ever since the AIADMK came to power, law and order have become a public laughing stock. News of a murder every day, a robbery every hour and a rape every week stings us like red scorpions. Yet the AIADMK government that should act stages a drama as though none of this concerns it and Tamil Nadu were a garden of peace, deceiving itself and the people. Yesterday was the debate on the Police Department grant. It is normally held over two days each year. The fact that it was reduced to one day shows how much democracy this government permits.

Replying to the debate, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa reportedly said, "Tamil Nadu is the State in which women are safest in all India." Perhaps, unable to say that men are safe here, she chose to say that women are safe. This letter presents examples of just how safe women are in Tamil Nadu.

Under AIADMK rule, two young girls staying at a school hostel in Pollachi were sexually assaulted. The list stretches from Vimala near Nannilam and the young woman Malar at Koyambedu to Vinitha, a Dalit student who had completed Class XII and was raped and murdered in a betel-vine field near Mayanur in Karur district.
Here is only a small list of those raped, or raped and murdered, in Tamil Nadu:

* Nine-year-old Gayathri of Andiyappan village near Tirupattur in Vellore district was sexually assaulted and murdered, 29 December 2012. * Nandini was raped and murdered in Sivagiri, 22 January 2013. * The body of a woman named Vijaya was found in Saidapet, reported by *Dina Thanthi*, 11 January 2013. * Punitha, a thirteen-year-old schoolgirl at Srivaikuntam in Thoothukudi district, was raped and murdered, *Dinamani*, 22 December 2012. * Two men raped an eleven-year-old Dalit girl in Nagapattinam district, *Dinamani*, 23 December 2012. * A young woman named Suganthi was raped on the bank of the Manimuthar at Virudhachalam, *Dinamani*, 27 December 2012. * Sandhiya, a young woman from Manjakuzhi near Chidambaram, was raped and thrown into the street from a third floor, *Theekkathir*, 27 December 2012. * Menaka, a married young woman of Thatharavalasai in Harur taluk, Dharmapuri, was raped and murdered, *Dinamani*, 30 December 2012. * Mariammal, separated from her husband and living with her father and three children on Second Street, Matha Nagar, Thoothukudi, was raped after an intruder entered the house before dawn, *Dinamani*, 29 December 2012. * Kalki, a schoolgirl from Rukmanipuram in Vanur, Villupuram, was raped and her body hung in a casuarina grove, *Dina Thanthi*, 5 January 2013. * A twelve-year-old girl was sexually assaulted in Udumalai and, unable to bear the humiliation, set herself on fire. * An eighteen-year-old woman was raped by a gang of four near the Collector's office in Namakkal, *Dina Thanthi*, 6 January 2013. * Abinaya, a seventeen-year-old from Timiri near Arcot in Vellore district, died by self-immolation after threats and intimidation while trying to protect herself, *Dina Thanthi*, 6 January 2013. * An intruder attempted to rape a Class XII student at her home in Mogappair, Ambattur, *Dina Thanthi*, 6 January 2013. * Premalatha was raped and murdered at Pozhichalur, Chennai, and her body thrown in a forest, *Dina Thanthi*, 6 January 2013. * Meena, a married young woman near Thottiyam in Tiruchi district, was abducted in a car and raped by four men while attending a relative's wedding. * Kannamma, a domestic worker at Koyambedu in Chennai, was raped by her employer, *Dina Thanthi*, 7 January 2013. * An eleven-year-old Class VI student tending goats at Pudur, Orathanadu, was raped, *Dina Thanthi*, 4 January 2013. * A young girl was murdered near Ranipet and her burnt body found in a cremation ground, *Dina Thanthi*, 13 January 2013. * A woman working at Selvi Xerox in Adambakkam, Chennai, was raped, *Dina Thanthi*, 4 January 2013. * A girl was sexually assaulted in Mylapore on 6 February 2013; the absconding driver's mother died by suicide. * A sixteen-year-old girl was abducted and sexually assaulted in Dharmapuri on 17 February 2013.
The list goes on without end.

On 25 June 2014, *The Hindu* carried a long article titled: "Crime against Women, especially against Dalits has been quite frequent in the Central Districts in the past few years; while the Police are tardy in registering complaints, the indifference of authorities to the plight of the marginalised sections continues - HURT and MUTE." It listed a nineteen-year-old student raped and murdered at Amur in February 2012; a twelve-year-old Dalit girl raped and murdered at Sirkazhi in March 2012; an eight-year-old Dalit girl raped at Thalaignayiru in Nagapattinam district in December 2012; a seventeen-year-old raped and murdered within Nagudi police limits in Pudukkottai district in January 2014; and an eight-year-old student subjected to sexual violence at Vedaranyam in April 2014.

Are sexual offences the only problem? On 20 June 2014, *The Times of India* reported extensively on robberies under AIADMK rule. Tamil Nadu police had still not found a diversionary-theft gang alleged to have stolen sovereigns of jewellery and more than twenty lakh rupees in over 180 incidents since 2013. As many as 85 such cases from 2013 remained unsolved, and not one of 25 diversionary thefts in Chennai had been solved.

Yet, opening a conference of District Collectors and police officers in Chennai, Jayalalithaa said law and order were in a condition in which everyone could feel pride and satisfaction. At the previous year's Collectors' conference too, she claimed that law, order and peace in Tamil Nadu were better than in any other State.

A few incidents are enough to show the actual condition. In November 2013, AIADMK MLA Karuppaiah of Sholavandan escaped with his life only by locking himself in a hotel room when a gang led by an AIADMK union secretary came to attack him with clubs near Vadipatti. Jayalalithaa blamed the DMDK for disorder in the Assembly, but forgot that AIADMK members too had caused disorder. In the first week of November 2013, newspapers reported nine murders in a single day across Tiruchi, Thanjavur and Nagapattinam.

In yesterday's reply, Jayalalithaa boasted that the many protests in Tamil Nadu did not mean people had been driven to desperation, but that her government generously granted permission under true democratic principles. Here are examples of that generosity.

Police denied the DMDK permission for a November 2013 public meeting at Cuddalore, citing law and order. On appeal, Justice Sasidharan ruled that the denial was wrong, that providing security was the police's duty, and that permission must be granted.

District authorities barred Dr Ramadoss from Madurai and Cuddalore districts, barred Viduthalai Chiruthaigal leader Thol. Thirumavalavan from Villupuram district, and imposed prohibitory orders under Section 144 across Dharmapuri district. Democratic activity was crushed.

The DMK applied on 30 January 2013 to hold a Salem parliamentary-constituency workers' meeting on 16 February with Treasurer M. K. Stalin as speaker. Permission was denied. On the DMK's petition, Sessions Judge Bhuvaneswari ordered that no one should prevent the meeting. The government also barred a Karur meeting Stalin was to attend on 15 December 2012; Justice S. Subbiah permitted it at the same venue. Permission was likewise denied for a Thoothukudi workers' meeting on 28 December 2012 until Justice Ravichandrababu allowed it. The basic democratic rights to speak and assemble were denied with an authoritarian mindset.

Not only DMK events were obstructed. Was permission granted for Superstar Rajinikanth's birthday event? Or actor Vijay's birthday meeting? Or the TESO conference? No. Permission had to be won in court.

*Dina Thanthi* reported "16 Murders in Chennai in One Month," noting that suspects in five had not been arrested and police had made no breakthrough in the sensational murder of a woman bank officer at Nungambakkam.

Here is another certificate awarded by the court to the police under Jayalalithaa: *The Hindu* reported on 14 February 2012 that the Madras High Court Bench came down heavily on officers "who think they are a law unto themselves just because they are vested with certain powers under the Code of Criminal Procedure and that the common people are at their mercy."

There would be neither enough time nor newspaper space to record every instance showing the failure to maintain law and order. Yet whether at Collectors' conferences, in replies to the Governor's Address, or in Police grant debates, Jayalalithaa habitually claims Tamil Nadu is a proud model of law and order, and newspapers print it in banner headlines. Does a lie become truth merely because it is repeated and advertised?
The documented total from newspapers during three years of AIADMK rule was 3,525 murders, 1,352 robberies, 1,023 waylaying and fraud cases, and 1,120 chain-snatching incidents.

Gandhiji said India would attain true freedom only when a woman wearing jewellery over her whole body could walk alone at midnight without harm. Sixty-seven years have passed since Independence. Has that dream been realised? Can a woman wearing jewellery walk at midnight? Even in broad daylight in Chennai we see reports of six chain-snatchings, nine chain-snatchings, and a woman's chain snatched opposite a police station. Is this their model of protection for women?

On 21 December 2012, *The Times of India* reported that more than 500 rape cases had been registered through September and that rape in Tamil Nadu had risen by nine per cent. The Tamil Nadu Police website recorded 484 cases from January through September 2011, against 528 in the same period of 2012, including 75 in Chennai alone. Is this how women are honoured?

The Chief Minister said 923 rape cases were filed in 2013. Is that evidence that crimes against women declined? She sought to explain the number away by saying other kinds of cases had been registered under that category. Who made that error? The police under her own authority. Does she seek to escape by blaming them?

In the case in which three women students of the Dharmapuri Agricultural College were burned to death inside a bus, the Sessions Judge said police had treated the case like an orphan because the accused belonged to the ruling party, destroying public trust in the police. The court censured the Home Secretary and the CB-CID Additional Superintendent of Police and directed disciplinary action for allowing prosecution witnesses to be dispersed. What empty words it is to burn women students and then speak of women's safety!

Even after a court directed action on Dr Rani's complaint against AIADMK MLA Paranjothi in Tiruchi, no action was taken for many days. The affected woman has still received no remedy under this government.

After Jayalalithaa left for Kodanadu on 1 January 2013, a long announcement appeared in her name: a thirteen-point programme to suppress crimes against women, accompanied by the usual praise and glossy advertising, as though she had already made Tamil Nadu free of sexual violence.

The first three points were merely administrative instructions about who would investigate new and pending sexual-violence cases. The fourth invoked the Goondas Act, already being misused against those the government disliked, who then had to seek justice from the courts. Another point promised women's fast-track courts in every district. Another promised medical expenses for survivors. What became of all thirteen points? Like so many announcements, did they remain no more useful than a vegetable drawn on paper?
With affection,
M. K.
8 August 2014
